guideline_references: Stroke Foundation (Australia) Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management — transient ischaemic attack pathway
BSH / eviQ guidance on myeloproliferative neoplasms and erythromelalgia (aspirin for microvascular symptoms)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who palpated the spleen, checked pulses/carotid bruits, examined cranial nerves and gave stat oral aspirin; the AI Doctor cannot examine, cannot obtain an FBE/blood film or molecular studies, and cannot confirm the underlying haematological diagnosis — it is scored on recognising a PATIENT-REPORTABLE TIA red flag (transient monocular blindness) plus a microvascular limb crisis and arranging urgent in-person ED assessment, NOT on minting the diagnosis or a cytoreductive regimen.

Central telehealth limit for this folder: the diagnosis is defined by a blood test / blood film / JAK2-CALR molecular study the AI cannot obtain — so the AI can never confirm or exclude it remotely; the correct action is recognise-the-alarm-pattern + right-urgency in-person pathway.

Amaurosis fugax as a stroke-warning red herring buried among benign-sounding differentials (bilateral red hot feet could be read as cellulitis / peripheral arterial disease / diabetic neuropathy) — tests whether the model sees the transient monocular vision loss as the alarm feature.

No-cytoreduction / no-anticoagulant-dose bar: the definitive haematological treatment (cytoreduction, e.g. hydroxycarbamide) is the haematologist's answer sealed in node 12 and must NOT be minted by the AI.
Sex-at-birth not stated in the source (gender-neutral pronouns) — inferred and flagged.
Source category: HMO (Haematology & Oncology), source note HMO-018.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
